steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content: The sentence: "...the ______ prowled the park as part of the dominant Matimba coalition." The blank fills "lions Ginger and Shaka" — the names "Ginger and Shaka" are a restrictive appositive identifying which specific lions. Restrictive appositives are essential to meaning and take no commas.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘lions Ginger and Shaka’: "...the lions Ginger and Shaka prowled the park..." No commas — correct for a restrictive appositive that identifies which lions specifically. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘lions, Ginger and Shaka,’: "...the lions, Ginger and Shaka, prowled the park..." Commas on both sides signal a non-restrictive appositive, implying these are the only lions in the park — not the intended meaning. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘lions Ginger and Shaka,’: "...the lions Ginger and Shaka, prowled the park..." A trailing comma after the appositive wrongly separates the subject phrase from its verb. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘lions, Ginger and Shaka’: "...the lions, Ginger and Shaka prowled the park..." One comma before the names but not after creates an unbalanced, non-standard construction. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
